Footnotes

1 Under graduated voting, the number of votes exercisable by a shareholder increases disproportionately less than the number of shares, while a capped voting scheme imposes an upper ceiling on the total number of votes a single shareholder can exercise regardless of the amount of stock held.

2 By contrast, blockholders in a minority position are not at risk of getting expropriated when a firm is run (by the board) in small shareholders’ interests. Since private benefits arguably only can be shared among a few parties, small shareholders want firm value to be maximized. Consequently, blockholders receive their fair pro rata share.

3 Shareholders have varying tax positions ( Eckbo and Verma 1994 ; Desai and Jin 2012 ) and varying exposures because of hedging ( Martin and Partnoy 2005 ). Institutional investors differ in their support for management ( Matvos and Ostrovsky 2010 ) and have varying time horizons ( Gaspar, Massa, and Matos 2005 ; Chen, Harford, and Li 2007 ). Further, shareholders may differ in their views because they represent particular constituencies, such as unions ( Matsusaka, Ozbas, and Yi 2019 ; Agrawal 2012 ) or public pension funds ( Romano 1993 ). Li, Maug, and Schwartz-Ziv (2019) and Bolton et al. (2020) show how shareholders’ voting patterns reflect disagreement between shareholders. Schwartz-Ziv and Volkova (2020) find that having multiple heterogeneous blockholders exacerbates disagreement in shareholder meetings.

4 A prominent example of boards intervening to protect minority shareholders’ interests against overreach by a majority shareholder is the proposed merger of CBS and Viacom by the controlling Redstone family (e.g., Stelter 2018 ).

5 Some of these data were collected in Ostergaard and Smith (2011) .

6 Listed stockholdings do not quite add to the total number of shares for five firms. We assume that unaccounted-for shares are held in stakes equal to the average stake of the listed shareholders. The fraction of unaccounted-for shares ranges from 0.3–12 percent (5 percent on average).

7 It is evident from the tasks given to the boards of directors that it is deeply involved in operations, including setting product prices and obtaining tender offers. See Appendix C .

8 In support of this conjecture, Amihud, Mendelson, and Uno (1999) find that minimum trading unit reductions for shares on the Tokyo Stock Exchange increase the number of individual shareholders. Furthermore, U.S. households tilt their investments toward low-priced and small-denomination stocks ( Kumar and Lee 2006 ; Barber and Odean 2000 ), and surveyed corporate managers consider stock price reductions, making stocks affordable to small investors, the primary motive for stock splits ( Baker and Gallagher 1980 ).

9 Because of missing observations, we estimate total debt and ROA as the average of firm-level observations over the 1896–1910 period. In this manner we estimate debt-to-total assets ratios for 43 firms, debt-equity ratios for 45 firms, and ROA for 41 firms.

10 Also, multiple blockholders are not isomorphic to one shareholder owning their collective equity stake because of, for example, a duplication in monitoring, bargaining frictions, or disagreement. But even with such frictions, a larger collective equity stake makes blockholders internalize more of the firm value, thereby aligning their interest better with those of the small shareholders.

11 Similar voting arrangements existed at the time in many other countries (e.g., Dunlavy 2004 ; Hannah 2007 ; Hilt 2008 ; Musacchio 2008 ) and persist in Dutch, French, and Swiss corporations. Today, voting caps are permitted in U.S. and U.K. corporate law, but not in German and Japanese law ( Kraakman et al. 2017 ).

12 The voting restrictions observed in our sample promote board representation of small shareholders, as well as overweighing their votes in all matters on which the GM decides. Such general empowerment provides greater protection than most modern director appointment rights, which seems called for in our setting without statutory minority shareholder protection.

13 A 2.5 % voting cap threshold (our calculation) means that the upper limit on votes kicks in at a point at which a shareholder owns a number of shares corresponding to 2.5 % of outstanding shares. In the Akers Mechanical Workshop example, the upper limit of 10 votes is reached when a shareholder owns 66 shares. This corresponds to 5.5 % of the 1,200 outstanding shares.

14 Bennedsen and Wolfenzon (2000) argue that a one-share-one-vote structure is optimal for firms with many blockholders. While they do not explicitly consider voting caps, such restrictions would be beneficial in their setting because they would force any controlling coalition of blockholders to hold an even larger equity stake, thereby aligning their interest better with those of the shareholders in the minority position.

15 See Yermack 2004 ; Fos and Tsoutsoura 2014 ; Brochet and Srinivasan 2014 .

16 For instance, Ferreira, Ferreira, and Mariano (2018) show that directors with links to the firm’s creditors push for creditor-friendly policies. Boards with a larger proportion of directors appointed subsequent to the CEO are more lenient monitors ( Coles, Naveen, and Naveen 2014 ; Wade, O’Reilly, and Chandratat 1990 ; Landier et al. 2013 ).

17 Boards are occasionally authorized to take the following decisions: Determining managers’ authority, setting the maximum amount for which managers can bind a firm ( procura ), resolving disputes among managers, deciding or approving minor business decisions (e.g., product prices), giving directions to the superintendent, electing the auditor, writing the instructions for the auditor, hiring the bookkeeper, hiring the treasurer, determining the amount of collateral posted by the treasurer.

18 Boards never have joint authority with GMs, which by itself is evidence of the mediation role.

19 GM protocols record these debates. For example, at the 1893 GM of Bægna Wood Sanding Company, a firm without a board, management proposes a 5 % dividend and to add 720 NOK to reserves. This proposal is rejected in favor of an alternative put forward by shareholder Bjørklund, involving a fixing of the level of dividends at 6 % in the statute (and consequently adding less to reserves in that year).

20 These theories are all mute on reasons for establishing boards. Therefore, reasons outside the models, for instance, conflicts between shareholders, must motivate boards.

21 A t -test shows that for 42 firms for which we have data on fixed and total assets, firms with above-median fixed-to-total asset ratios have an average debt-to-equity ratio of 89 % compared with 43 % for below-median firms. The difference is significant at the 16 % level.

22 Norges Kommunale Finantser.

With civil servants as the regressor, and in Table 12 , the results are unaffected when Bergen is omitted.
